policy_statement: Involve the media in communicating health issues and climate change impacts to the public.
mechanism_description: Using media and public messaging increases awareness of climate-related health risks, which supports better public preparedness and adaptive response.
typology_evidence_quote: Increase public awareness of climate changes effects and impacts on general health. ... Involve the media in carrying the message
primary_causal_pathway: Information and risk communication → ↑ awareness/preparedness/adaptive capacity → ↓ vulnerability to climate hazards
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) The policy changes how climate and health risk information is communicated to the public, typically through media campaigns, alerts, outreach, and public education. (2) The mediating process is behavioral and institutional: better-informed individuals and communities recognize risks earlier, take protective actions, and coordinate more effectively with health and emergency systems. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is reduced exposure, sensitivity, and harm from climate-related health impacts such as heat stress, air-quality events, disease risks, or extreme weather disruptions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is to reduce harm from climate-related hazards by increasing public preparedness, awareness, and adaptive response capacity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
(1) Dominant causal pathway: the mechanism operates by improving risk awareness and preparedness, which increases adaptive capacity and reduces vulnerability to climate-related health hazards.  
(2) Why this primary over alternatives: it is not primarily Mitigation because the mechanism does not directly reduce greenhouse gas emissions; it is not Resource Efficiency because the central effect is not delivering the same service with fewer material or energy inputs; it is not Nature-Based Solutions because no ecosystem restoration or conservation process is involved.  
(3) Edge cases applied: although communication campaigns can sometimes produce mitigation co-benefits if they influence low-carbon behavior, this canonical mechanism is specifically framed around climate-related health risks, preparedness, and adaptive response, so adaptation clearly dominates.  
(4) Applicability to all instances: for policies sharing this mechanism, the consistent climate-relevant effect is stronger public readiness and response to climate hazards, so Adaptation remains the primary category across instances.  
(5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence, since the exact hazard focus (e.g., heat, flooding, disease, air quality) and implementation details may vary by policy.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Possible overlap with mitigation-focused awareness campaigns exists in other communication mechanisms, but this mechanism is explicitly tied to climate-risk preparedness and adaptive response, making Adaptation the clear primary classification.
key_indicators: “Involve the media”; “carrying the message”; “issues of Health”; “climate changes effects and impacts on general health”; “Increase public awareness”; “advertisement and social media sites”
co_benefits: Improved general public health awareness and risk literacy beyond climate-specific hazards; stronger public engagement with health messaging.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also resemble a planning-strategy because it implies a communications approach, but the operative action is public dissemination of information through media channels, so information-disclosure is the better fit.
